---
title: "Why a software engineer uses Quarto"
description: "It came out of the R world, but it's the cleanest documentation-plus-website pipeline I've found."
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-05-27"
categories: [tooling, writing]
draft: true
---

This site is built with Quarto. So are my lab-meeting slides, my dissertation figures, and the notes I keep while reading papers. I came to it from thirty years of software and no R background at all, which makes me an outlier in its user base — and a decent test of whether the tool earns its keep outside the community that built it.

## What it is

Quarto is a publishing system built on Pandoc. You write plain-text `.qmd` files — markdown, plus a YAML header, plus executable code blocks — and Quarto renders them to HTML, PDF, Word, slides, whole websites, or books. It's the successor to R Markdown, but language-agnostic: R, Python, Julia, and Observable JavaScript all run natively in the same document.

## Why the R community leans on it

Quarto comes from Posit (the company formerly called RStudio), and it inherits the lineage of knitr and R Markdown — fifteen years of literate-programming culture where a report, its code, and its figures live in one reproducible source. For an R user, Quarto is the natural next step from a workflow they already trust, with deep integration into the RStudio IDE. The gravity is real and historical.

## Why it's useful to me, a Python person

Two reasons.

First, it's a rigorous documentation pipeline. Code, prose, math, and citations live in one plain-text file that renders deterministically. A figure generated by a Python block can't drift out of sync with the paragraph describing it, because the paragraph reruns the code. `freeze: true` caches expensive computation, so a rebuild only re-runs what changed. The source is version-controllable, diffable, and survives software churn in a way a Jupyter notebook or a Word document does not.

Second, it's a website builder that gets out of the way. This site is a directory of `.qmd` files, a `_quarto.yml`, and a `git push` to GitHub Pages. No JavaScript framework, no build step I have to babysit, no CMS. The same `.qmd` I write for the blog can render to a PDF or a reveal.js slide deck without my touching the prose. One source, many outputs.

That second point is the one that converted me. I wanted a single source of truth for technical writing — something that produces a paper, a talk, and a web page from the same file, with the code that made the figures embedded in it. Quarto is the only thing I've found that does all of that without fighting me.

## How to start

Install the [Quarto CLI](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/), then:

```bash
quarto create project website mysite
cd mysite
quarto preview     # live-reloading local server
quarto render      # build the site into _site/
```

A `.qmd` file is markdown with a YAML header and optional code chunks. If you've written a Jupyter notebook or a README, you already know most of it.

## Worth reading and watching

- [Quarto documentation](https://quarto.org) — comprehensive, well-organised, the rare docs that are a pleasure to use.
- [Get Started guide](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/) — install plus a first project, with separate paths for VS Code, Jupyter, RStudio, and the terminal.
- ["Hello Quarto" keynote](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=p7Hxu4coDl8) (Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel & Julia Stewart Lowndes, rstudio::conf 2022) — the best hour-long overview of what the tool is for.
- ["Get started with Quarto"](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_f3latmOhew) — Mine's shorter hands-on walkthrough.
- [Quarto gallery](https://quarto.org/docs/gallery/) — websites, books, dashboards, and slides built with it, all with source.

If you write anything technical and keep the code somewhere separate from the prose, Quarto is worth a weekend.

---

*AI generated content with human revision.*
